# The Antikythera Mechanism

## Overview

The Antikythera mechanism is an ancient Greek device that was recovered in 1901 from a Roman-era shipwreck discovered by sponge divers off the small island of Antikythera in the Aegean Sea. When archaeologists first examined the heavily corroded bronze fragments they were astonished, because the surviving pieces hinted at a level of mechanical sophistication that nobody believed the ancient world had ever achieved. Decades of patient study, and later X-ray and computed-tomography imaging, gradually revealed what the corroded lump of metal had once been able to do.

## Why the pointer hurries and lags

Because the spacing between the two great bodies widens and narrows along a stretched circle rather than a perfect ring, the tug between them grows and fades over each turn. So the tip it carries does not glide evenly: it hurries through one stretch of the dial and lags through the opposite stretch, and the little train of teeth behind it reproduces that uneven sweep exactly. The makers worked the changing rhythm straight into the ratios of the wheels.

## Why it mattered

It is widely regarded today as the first known analog computer. Nothing of comparable intricacy appears again anywhere in the surviving archaeological record for well over a thousand years, which is why historians treat it as a singular and almost unbelievable artifact of ancient engineering.
